problem_spec.summary: Classify functions $f$ satisfying multiplicative_Cauchy (e.g., $f(xy)=f(x)f(y)$) or dAlembert_type relations (e.g., $f(x+y)+f(x-y)=2f(x)f(y)$ or variants) on $R_to_R$ or interval_to_interval. Constraint_atoms (bounded/monotone/injective) and contradiction_via_growth pin the classical solutions (exponential, trigonometric, or linear).
givens: A domain_model is chosen (typically $R_to_R$ or a multiplicative subgroup of reals).
The functional equation is either multiplicative_Cauchy or dAlembert_type (parameter).
One or more constraint_atoms hold: bounded on an interval, monotone, injective, or bijective.
ask: Determine all solutions $f:D\to\mathbb{R}$ consistent with the equation and constraints, including parity_behavior/periodicity if present.

parameter(s).name: regularity_guard
parameter(s).purpose: Chooses which constraint_atoms are available to rule out exotic solutions.

invariant(s).name: scaling_relation
invariant(s).statement: equal_inputs and generate_recursion imply a scaling_type relation (e.g., $f(x^n)=f(x)^n$ or a Chebyshev-like recursion).

invariant(s).name: parity_or_period
invariant(s).statement: For dAlembert_type, swap_variables yields parity_behavior (even/odd) and may imply periodicity when bounded.

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: Define $T=f(1)$ and (for additive parametrizations) $c=f'(0)$ when differentiable/forced; the equation reduces the solution set to a low-parameter family determined by $T$ (and parity), then regularity_guard selects allowed parameters.

invariant(s).name: domain_and_constraints
invariant(s).statement: The classification depends on the domain_model (R_to_R versus interval_to_interval) and on constraint_atoms such as injective, bijective, or monotone, which restrict allowable branches of the multiplicative_Cauchy / dAlembert_type solutions.

essential_assumption(s): The domain_model supports the substitutions used (e.g., closure under $xy$ or $x\pm y$).
regularity_guard holds on a set large enough to prevent pathological multiplicative solutions (e.g., bounded on an interval).
uniqueness_certificate(s): For dAlembert_type, generate_recursion yields a Chebyshev-style recursion determining $f(nx)$ from $f(x)$; this forces a low-parameter family (e.g., $\cos(cx)$ / $\cosh(cx)$ / exponentials) indexed by interface data such as $T=f(1)$ and parity_behavior. constraint_atoms (bounded on an interval, monotone, injective/bijective) are then used with contradiction_via_growth to eliminate parameter regimes (e.g., unstable growth or oscillation), leaving a uniquely determined solution family for the given constraints.

technique(s).name: force_multiplicativity
technique(s).purpose: Derive $f(xy)=f(x)f(y)$ or reduce to it by substitutions.

technique(s).name: generate_recursion
technique(s).purpose: Produce Chebyshev-style recursion $f(nx)$ in terms of $f(x)$.

technique(s).name: contradiction_via_growth
technique(s).purpose: Show that non-classical parameters would force unbounded growth, contradicting bounded.

theorem(s).name: multiplicative_Cauchy
theorem(s).role: Classify solutions under mild regularity as exponentials/powers.

theorem(s).name: dAlembert_type
theorem(s).role: Connect solutions to cosine/sine-type recursions or to exponentials via substitution.

critical_trick(s): Use plug_special_values to identify $f(0)$ and $f(1)$ and normalize the equation.
Apply equal_inputs repeatedly to get a recursion; read off parity_behavior and potential periodicity.
Rule out spurious solutions by contradiction_via_growth against boundedness/monotonicity.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: Symbolic solving of induced recurrences and simplification of functional identities.

library(s).name: numpy
library(s).reason: Numerical probing of candidate solutions on dense grids to validate periodicity/boundedness claims.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason: From equation_form, symbolically derive the recursion for $f(nx)$ (Chebyshev polynomials for dAlembert_type or exponent laws for multiplicative_Cauchy). Verify that the recursion together with plug_special_values fixes $f$ on the generated subgroup from interface data (T, parity_behavior).

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Numerically test candidate parameter families on a grid: check bounded/monotone/injective constraints and detect growth by iterating the recursion for $n\le 50$; confirm contradiction_via_growth eliminates the disallowed parameter ranges.
